docs.title: Australian tax residence guide
docs.text: https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 1 of 12 Understanding tax residency status for Tax Purposes in Australia May 29, 2026 Your tax residency status is one of the most consequential determinations the ATO will make about you. Get it right and you know exactly what you owe. Get it wrong and you could face unexpected bills, penalties, or miss out on offsets you were entitled to claim. The core issue is straightforward: Australian tax residents pay tax on their worldwide income. Non-residents pay tax only on income sourced within Australia. That single distinction can mean thousands of dollars of difference in your annual tax position, especially if you have overseas income, assets held abroad, or a period of working internationally. The Australian Taxation Office ( (https://www.ato.gov.au/) ATO ) uses a series of statutory tests to determine where you sit. Understanding those tests, and how they apply to your personal circumstances, is the starting point for getting this right. What Is the Importance of Tax Residency Status? Tax residency status determines the full scope of your Australian tax obligations. Residents are assessed on all income, regardless of where in the world it was earned. That includes salary from a foreign employer, rental income from an overseas property, and returns from international investments. Non-residents face a narrower obligation but are generally taxed at higher rates on their Australian-sourced income, and they lose access to the tax-free threshold. So while a non- resident might owe no Australian tax on foreign income, they will pay more on whatever they do earn here. Residency status also affects your eligibility for the Medicare levy, the foreign income tax offset, and certain capital gains tax concessions. For anyone moving to Australia, leaving the country, or splitting time between two jurisdictions, getting the right classification early avoids compounding problems at lodgement time. The tax (https://www.bentleys.com.au) accountants at Bentleys https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 2 of 12 regularly help clients work through these questions, particularly where the facts are complex or the outcome is disputed. Key Tests for Determining Your Tax Residency Status The ATO does not rely on a single test to classify your residency. It applies to up to four statutory tests in sequence. If you satisfy any one of them, you are an Australian tax resident for that financial year. The tests are the Resides Test (the primary test), the Domicile Test, the 183-Day Test, and the Commonwealth Superannuation Test. Each looks at different aspects of your connection to Australia, from your lifestyle and living arrangements to your physical presence and employment status. They work independently. Failing one does not prevent you from satisfying another. The Resides Test The Resides Test is the starting point and asks a basic question: does this person live in Australia? The answer requires more than counting nights spent here. The ATO looks at the totality of your circumstances to decide whether Australia is genuinely where you reside. Factors considered include where your family lives, where you are employed, whether you own or rent property in Australia, your participation in community or social activities, and how regularly you return here. No single factor is determinative. Someone who splits their time between Australia and another country might still pass the Resides Test if the weight of their personal and economic ties is firmly here. If the Resides Test is satisfied, you are a resident. If it is not, the ATO moves to the remaining tests. The Domicile Test for Tax The Domicile Test applies where someone does not appear to reside in Australia in the ordinary sense but may still have Australia as their permanent home. It is particularly relevant for Australians living or working overseas temporarily, such as those on an expatriate assignment or posted abroad for work. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 3 of 12 Under the Domicile Test, you are an Australian tax resident if your domicile (your permanent home) is in Australia, unless the ATO is satisfied that your permanent place of abode is outside Australia. That last qualification matters. If you have moved overseas and established a genuine long-term home there, the Domicile Test may not catch you, even if you were born in Australia or hold an Australian passport. Property ownership, family ties, where your assets are held, and the duration and intent of your overseas stay all feed into this assessment. The 183-Day Rule Australia The 183-Day Test provides a more mechanical trigger. Spend 183 days or more in Australia during a financial year and you will generally be classified as a tax resident for that year, even without any intention of making Australia your permanent home. The count runs across the full financial year (1 July to 30 June). Days of arrival and departure are typically counted as full days. The test can catch people who have been in Australia on an extended work project, even without any plan to stay permanently. There is an exception. If you can demonstrate that your usual place of abode is outside Australia and you had no intention of taking up residence here, the 183-day count alone may not be enough to classify you as a resident. But this exception is applied narrowly. If you are spending that much time in Australia, the ATO will examine your circumstances closely. The Permanent Place of Abode The Permanent Place of Abode test runs alongside the Domicile Test rather than operating as a standalone measure. It is the mechanism that prevents someone from being treated as an Australian tax resident purely because their domicile is here, where they have genuinely established a long-term home overseas. To satisfy this exemption, your overseas home must be more than temporary accommodation. A short-term rental while on contract work abroad would generally not qualify. A home you have bought, furnished, and settled into with your family, with no immediate intention to return to Australia, is more likely to meet the standard. Factors the ATO considers include the duration of your overseas stay, whether you have sold or rented out your Australian property, where your spouse and children are living, and whether you maintain social and financial ties within Australia. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 4 of 12 The Commonwealth Superannuation Test The Commonwealth Superannuation Test is a narrow test that applies only to Australian government employees posted overseas. If you are a member of the Public Sector Superannuation Scheme, the Commonwealth Superannuation Scheme, or a similar government fund, and you are working abroad, you may be classified as an Australian tax resident under this test regardless of your physical location. The policy reasoning is sensible. Government employees working overseas are there at the direction of the Australian Government, not because they have chosen to relocate. Treating them as non-residents would create inconsistencies relative to colleagues working within Australia. Government employees and contractors should take advice on how this test interacts with their broader tax position, including superannuation planning. Bentleys’ (https://www.bentleys.com.au/services/tax-advisory/) tax advisors work with clients in both public and private sectors on these cross-border arrangements. Special Cases and Exemptions Working Holiday Makers Working holiday makers, those who hold a subclass 417 or 462 visa, are taxed at a flat rate on their Australian income rather than the progressive rates applied to residents. From the 2017 financial year onwards, that rate has been 15% on the first $45,000 of taxable income, with ordinary non-resident rates applying above that threshold. Working holiday makers are generally treated as non-residents for tax purposes, regardless of how many days they spend in Australia, and they do not have access to the tax-free threshold. Temporary Residents Temporary residents enjoy a partial exemption from Australian tax. If you hold a temporary visa and are not the spouse of an Australian citizen or permanent resident, you are generally taxed only on your Australian-sourced income and employment income attributable to your time here. Most foreign income, including overseas interest, dividends, and capital gains from foreign assets, is excluded from your assessable Australian income. The temporary resident regime is a deliberate concession, recognising that people here on a provisional basis have not fully committed to the country and should not carry the same tax obligations as someone who has settled permanently. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 5 of 12 Part-Year Residency If your residency status changes during a financial year, different rules apply to each segment of the year. When you become an Australian tax resident, you are assessed as a resident from that date. When you cease to be a resident, non-resident treatment applies from that point. The ATO has specific guidance on how to handle income allocation and asset disposal timing in these situations, and getting the timing right can have a meaningful effect on your tax bill. The Impact of Tax Residency on Your Taxes The headline impact is straightforward. Residents pay tax on global income at progressive Australian rates, the rate for 2025-26 is 16 cents in the dollar on income from $18,201 to $45,000, following the Stage 3 tax cuts that took effect from 1 July 2024. Non-residents pay no Australian tax on foreign income but face higher flat rates on Australian income, with no tax- free threshold applying. The Medicare levy (currently 2% of taxable income) applies to residents but not non-residents. Higher-income residents without private hospital cover may also face the Medicare levy surcharge. For capital gains tax purposes, non-residents are only subject to CGT on “taxable Australian property”, primarily Australian real estate and interests in entities with predominantly real property assets. The 50% CGT discount that residents can access after holding an asset for 12 months is generally not available to foreign residents. These differences add up quickly. For someone with significant global income or a large property portfolio, the resident/non-resident distinction could mean tens of thousands of dollars per year. It is also worth noting that withholding tax obligations can arise when Australian entities make payments such as interest, dividends, or royalties to foreign residents. Bentleys’ (https://www.bentleys.com.au/client-alert-tax-news-september-2024/) September 2024 client alert on withholding for foreign residents explains these obligations in more detail, including when exemptions might apply. Double Taxation Agreements and Foreign Income Tax Offset Australia has signed double taxation agreements (DTAs) with over 40 countries. These agreements allocate taxing rights over specific income types so that the same income is not taxed in full by two countries simultaneously. DTAs commonly cover salary, dividends, royalties, pensions, and capital gains, specifying which country has the primary right to tax each type and at what rate. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 6 of 12 If you are an Australian resident who has already paid tax overseas on foreign income, you may be able to claim the foreign income tax offset (FITO) to reduce your Australian tax liability. The offset is generally capped at the lesser of the foreign tax paid and the Australian tax that would otherwise be payable on that income. It removes double taxation without resulting in a refund exceeding what you paid abroad. DTAs can also assist with residency determination itself. Some agreements include tie-breaker rules that determine which country a person is a tax resident of when both countries would otherwise claim them. If you are living or working in a DTA country and your residency status is uncertain, the relevant agreement is worth examining carefully. The (https://www.ato.gov.au/individuals-and-families/coming-to-australia-or-going-overseas/your-tax-residency) ATO’s guidance on tax residency for individuals includes information on how DTAs interact with the Australian tests. Tax Residency Self-Assessment Australia operates on a self-assessment basis. You determine your own tax residency status, and you are responsible for the accuracy of that determination. The ATO does not pre-approve your classification before you lodge your return. That creates a genuine record-keeping obligation. If you are claiming non-residency, you should be able to document your time spent outside Australia, your overseas living arrangements, and the nature of your ties to other countries. Travel records, lease agreements, employment contracts, and bank statements are all potentially relevant. The ATO does audit residency claims and tends to scrutinise arrangements that look contrived. A common mistake is assuming that simply being outside Australia for a period makes you a non-resident, particularly where you have maintained a home here, kept your family in Australia, or continued to earn Australian income. If your circumstances are anything other than clear-cut, seeking advice before lodgement is a better investment than addressing an ATO amendment after the fact. Residency Status Impact on Tax Rates For most people, the difference in tax rates between residents and non-residents is material. Residents have access to offsets, deductions, and concessions, including the low income tax offset and the seniors and pensioners tax offset, that non-residents cannot claim. Residents also benefit from the tax-free threshold and access to the CGT discount on eligible assets. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 7 of 12 For those in a borderline situation, getting the classification right matters in both directions. Claiming non-resident status when you are actually a resident creates a risk of penalties and interest if the ATO disagrees. Claiming resident status when you are actually a non-resident could mean overpaying tax unnecessarily. Bentleys’ (https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/international-tax-guide-for-foreign-companies-doing-business-in- australia/) guide to international tax for individuals and foreign companies explores how Australian tax rates and obligations apply across different residency classifications and business structures. Where to Go From Here Tax residency is not a set-and-forget classification. Your status can change when you leave Australia, return from overseas, change employment, sell property, or alter your living arrangements significantly. Reviewing your position whenever there is a material change in your circumstances keeps you ahead of any problems. If you are confident in your classification, keep documentation that would support it through an audit. If there is any uncertainty, professional advice before lodgement is cheaper and less stressful than dealing with an ATO review after the fact. The (https://www.ato.gov.au/individuals-and-families/coming-to-australia-or-going-overseas/your-tax-residency) ATO’s residency determination too l is a useful starting point for straightforward cases. For anything involving multiple countries, foreign assets, or recent changes in your living arrangements, a conversation with a specialist tax adviser will give you considerably more certainty. Disclaimer: This information is general in nature and should not be relied on as advice. It does not take into account the objectives, financial situation or needs of any particular person. You need to consider your financial situation and needs and seek professional advice before making any decisions based on this information. FAQs Am I an Australian resident for tax purposes? https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 8 of 12 You are generally considered an Australian resident for tax purposes if you satisfy at least one of the ATO’s residency tests, including the resides test, domicile test, 183-day test, or Commonwealth superannuation test. What is the difference between tax residency and immigration status? Tax residency is separate from immigration status. You can be a temporary visa holder but still be an Australian tax resident, or hold permanent residency while remaining a foreign resident for tax purposes. How does the resides test work in Australia? The resides test examines whether you live in Australia according to ordinary concepts, considering factors such as family ties, lifestyle, work arrangements, and where your assets are located. What is the 183-day test for Australian tax? If you spend 183 days or more in Australia during a financial year, you may be considered a tax resident unless your usual place of abode is overseas and you do not intend to reside in Australia. Do I need to pay tax on my worldwide income as an Australian resident? Yes, Australian tax residents generally must declare all worldwide income, including overseas salary, investments, and foreign rental income. What are the tax rates for foreign residents in Australia? For the 2025–26 financial year, foreign residents are taxed at 30 per cent up to $135,000, 37 per cent between $135,001 and $190,000, and 45 per cent above $190,000. Is there a tax-free threshold for Australian tax residents? Yes, Australian residents receive the standard tax-free threshold of $18,200, though this may be adjusted for part-year residency. What is the domicile test for Australian residency? You may be considered a resident if your legal domicile is in Australia unless you establish a permanent place of abode outside Australia. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 9 of 12 What constitutes a permanent place of abode outside Australia? The ATO generally looks for a settled, long-term overseas living arrangement with continuity and permanence rather than temporary accommodation. How does being a temporary resident affect my Australian tax? Temporary residents are generally only taxed on Australian-sourced income and are exempt from tax on most foreign income. Do working holiday makers count as Australian residents? Most working holiday makers are taxed under special rates starting at 15 per cent on the first $45,000 of Australian income regardless of residency status. How is the financial year defined for Australian tax residency? The Australian financial year runs from 1 July to 30 June, and residency tests are applied across this period. Does owning property in Australia make me a tax resident? Owning property alone does not determine residency, though it is one factor the ATO considers when assessing your overall circumstances. What is the Commonwealth superannuation test? This test applies to certain Australian Government employees and members of eligible Commonwealth superannuation schemes working overseas. Can I be a resident of two countries for tax purposes? Yes, dual residency is possible. In these cases, double taxation agreements usually contain tie- breaker rules to determine which country has primary taxing rights. Do foreign residents pay the Medicare levy? Foreign residents are generally exempt from the Medicare levy if they are not entitled to Medicare benefits. What happens if my residency status changes mid-year? https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 10 of 12 You may become a part-year resident, meaning different tax rules apply to different portions of the financial year. Is a holiday house in Australia considered a permanent place of abode? Generally not. Holiday homes usually lack the permanence and continuity required to establish residency for tax purposes. How does the ATO view short-term work contracts for residency? Short-term contracts alone do not automatically create residency, especially if you maintain stronger personal and financial ties overseas. Does the 183-day test apply to a calendar year? No, the test applies specifically to the Australian financial year from 1 July to 30 June. Will my overseas student debt affect my residency status? No, HELP or student debt does not determine residency, though Australian residents living overseas must still report worldwide income for repayment purposes. Do I need to inform the ATO when I leave Australia permanently? Yes, you should notify the ATO when your residency status changes, often through your final Australian tax return. How is Capital Gains Tax (CGT) applied to foreign residents? Foreign residents are generally only taxed on gains relating to taxable Australian property, such as Australian real estate. Can I use the ATO residency tool for a definitive answer? The ATO residency tool provides guidance only. Complex cases often require advice from a qualified Australian tax professional. Are there new residency laws coming to Australia? The Government has proposed reforms to modernise residency tests, though the current four statutory tests remain in force as of 2026. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 11 of 12 Does spending 183 days in Australia automatically make me a resident? Not automatically. You may still argue that your usual place of abode remains overseas and that you had no intention of residing permanently in Australia. Are temporary residents taxed on their foreign income? Generally, no. Most foreign investment income earned by temporary residents is excluded from Australian assessable income. What happens if my residency status changes partway through the year? A split-year treatment applies, meaning different tax rules and thresholds apply to resident and non-resident periods separately. Does tax residency match my visa or immigration status? No, Australian tax residency is determined independently from immigration law and is based on the statutory residency tests applied by the ATO. https://www.bentleys.com.au/insights/understanding-tax-residency-status-for-tax-purposes-in-australia/ 19/6/2026, 12:29 Page 12 of 12

docs.title: Brain tissue dna extraction method
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Extracting and preparing DNA from brain tissue for Oxford Nanopore Sequencing FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Extracting and preparing DNA from brain tissue for Oxford Nanopore Sequencing PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Method 1. Tissue lysis 2. DNA extraction 3. DNA shearing Results OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Extracting and preparing DNA from brain tissue for Oxford Nanopore Sequencing PAGE 2 Introduction This protocol describes a method to extract genomic DNA from human brain tissue using the QIAGEN MagAttract HMW DNA Kit and can be automated by using the KingFisher Flex or Duo Prime systems using the MagAttract handbook for these systems. This extraction method recovers ~8-15 µg of DNA from 25 mg of human brain tissue. Sequencing libraries were prepared using the Ligation Sequencing Kit both with and without a high throughput DNA shearing step using the FastPrep-96, before sequencing on PromethION Flow Cells. Flow cell output was increased by washing the flow cell using the Flow Cell Wash Kit and re-loading prepared library. Materials 25 mg of frozen brain tissue Ligation Sequencing Kit QIAGEN MagAttract HWM DNA Kit Scalpel Weighing boats 1 ml 96-well plates Plate sealer (e.g. Bio-Rad PX1 PCR plate sealer) Pierceable Foil Heat Seals 2 ml DNA LoBind Eppendorf Tubes P1000 pipette and tips P1000 wide-bore tips Weighing scales capable of measuring 25 mg Thermomixer Magnetic rack FastPrep (e.g. FastPrep-96 Classic bead beating grinder and lysis system) Optional materials QIAGEN TissueRuptor II 15 ml Falcon Tube Agilent Femto Pulse System Femto Pulse System DNA Analysis Kit (e.g. Genomic DNA 165 kb Kit) Method Tissue lysis Brain tissue should always be kept frozen prior to processing the sample. Please note, when the frozen sample is sliced, it will thaw quickly and generate condensation around the tissue, which will affect the weight. Please take the necessary precautions depending on the type and origin of the tissues you are working with. 1. Use a scalpel to thinly slice 25 mg of frozen brain tissue and place in a sterile weighing boat. 2. Homogenise the tissue to ensure adequate penetration of the lysis buffer into the tissue to accelerate the lysis step. This can be performed in one of two ways: OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Extracting and preparing DNA from brain tissue for Oxford Nanopore Sequencing PAGE 3 Recommended: Use a scalpel to mince the brain tissue in a weighing boat until it turns into a paste-like consistency which should take several minutes. Transfer the minced tissue into a 2 ml DNA LoBind Eppendorf Tube or 1 ml 96-well plate. Add 400 µl of MagAttract Lysis buffer (ATL) (MagAttract HMW DNA Kit) and 40 µl of proteinase K to the sample immediately. To ensure even dispersion through the sample, slowly mix by pipetting, using a P1000 pipette and standard P1000 tip approximately 5 times until the tissue is homogenised in the solution. Alternative (using TissueRuptor): Place 25 mg of sliced tissue in a 15 ml Falcon Tube and add 400 µl of MagAttract Lysis buffer (ATL). Shear the sample for 2 seconds at minimum speed in the TissueRuptor. Add 40 µl of proteinase K to the sample. 3. Place the tubes or 96-well plate in a thermomixer preheated to 56°C and shake at 1000 rpm for 1 hour. 4. Remove the samples from the thermomixer. Note: The tissue should be completely or almost completely dissolved. If not, it is possible the tissue was not properly broken down in the previous step. To dissolve any intact tissue, pipette mix the tissue using a P1000 pipette tip to break up any tissue aggregates and incubate for another 30-60 minutes. If some small pieces of tissue are still visible in solution after the lysis incubation, precipitate these by centrifuging at 16,000 x g for 3 minutes and remove any supernatant without disturbing the pellet. This is important as any undissolved pieces of tissue may bind to the MagAttract beads which can be very difficult to remove. 5. Take forward the homogenate to extract the DNA using a modified version of the QIAGEN MagAttract protocol. DNA extraction Note: This is a modified version of the QIAGEN Magattract HMW DNA protocol. 1. Add 8 µl of RNase and mix gently by flicking the tube. 2. Incubate at room temperature for 2 minutes. 3. Add 300 µl of AL buffer and mix using a P1000 wide-bore pipette 5-10 times. 4. Add 560 µl of MB buffer followed by 60 µl of MagAttract beads. Thoroughly resuspend by vortexing for 2-3 seconds. If using a 96-well plate, cover with adhesive film or use a plate sealer. 5. Incubate in a thermomixer for 3 minutes at 1000 rpm at room temperature. 6. Place the plate/tube on a magnet to pellet the magnetic beads. Remove the supernatant by pipetting without disturbing the bead pellet. 7. Add 700 µl of MW1 and resuspend the beads. 8. Use a thermomixer to mix the reaction for 2 minutes at 1000 rpm at room temperature. 9. Place the plate/tube on a magnet to pellet the magnetic beads. Remove the supernatant by pipetting without disturbing the bead pellet. 10. Repeat steps 7, 8 and 9 to complete a second wash with MW1. 11. Pre-warm Buffer AE to 80°C. 12. Add 700 µl of Buffer PE to the bead pellet. 13. Use a thermomixer to mix the reaction for 2 minutes at 1000 rpm at room temperature. 14. Place the plate/tube on a magnet to pellet the magnetic beads. Remove the supernatant by pipetting without disturbing the bead pellet. 15. Repeat steps 12, 13 and 14 to complete a second wash with Buffer PE. 16. Resuspend the beads with 150-200 µl Buffer AE, which has been pre-heated to 80°C, to elute the DNA. 17. Use a thermomixer to mix for 1 hour at 600 rpm at 37°C. 18. Place the plate/tube on a magnet to pellet the magnetic beads. Retain the supernatant containing the eluted DNA. 19. Quantify the recovered DNA using a Qubit fluorometer. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Extracting and preparing DNA from brain tissue for Oxford Nanopore Sequencing PAGE 4 DNA shearing 1. After recovering and quantifying the elution from the MagAttract beads, transfer the eluate into a new 1 ml 96-well plate. It is important to keep the volumes used in this shearing method constant as different volumes and different plates may have an impact on shearing performance. It is also advisable to avoid the formation of bubbles when transferring the DNA sample as bubbles may effect the shearing and impact the final output. Note: We recommend the plates are thoroughly sealed using a heat sealer and appropriate seal. 2. Place the sealed plate in the FastPrep-96. 3. Shear at 1200 rpm for 1 minute. 4. Remove the plate and spin down to make sure no liquid is left in the seal. 5. Optional: Evaluate the shearing by analysing an aliquot before and after shearing using an Agilent Femto Pulse (or equivalent for QC). 6. After the extraction and shearing step, proceed with the library preparation protocol for the Ligation Sequencing Kit. Results Figure 1. a) Read length analysis, b) output analysis and c) Femto Pulse analysis of sheared and unsheared gDNA extracted from human brain samples. Version Change V3, August 2023 Updated URL addresses V2, July 2022 Reformatted images V1, July 2022 Initial publication OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Extracting and preparing DNA from brain tissue for Oxford Nanopore Sequencing PAGE 5

docs.title: Buffy coat dna extraction method
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Buffy coat DNA FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Buffy coat DNA PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Method Results Sequencing performance Change log OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Buffy coat DNA PAGE 2 Introduction This protocol describes a method to extract high molecular weight genomic DNA from buffy coat, which has been isolated from rabbit blood, as an example of mammalian blood. The buffy coat is the fraction of an anticoagulated blood that contains white blood cells and platelets following centrifugation. Sequencing performance was determined by a MinION™ using the Ligation Sequencing Kit. Prior to library preparation, 3 µg of extracted DNA was size selected using SPRI beads. Materials 10 ml rabbit blood (Note: The buffy coat should be isolated, as soon as possible after blood collection) 15 and 50 ml Falcon tubes 1x phosphate buffer saline (PBS) Ficoll®-Paque PREMIUM Centrifuge (with adjustable deceleration) with capacity for 15 and 50 ml Falcon tubes QIAGEN MagAttract HMW DNA kit Eppendorf ThermoMixer 1.5 and 2 ml Eppendorf tubes Magnetic rack Vortex TE (10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) Method 1. If the blood has been stored after collection (e.g. at 4°C, -20°C or -80°C), allow it to equilibrate at room temperature for ~2 hours. Note: The buffy coat should be isolated via the process below, as soon as possible after blood collection. 2. Add 10 ml of blood to a 50 ml Falcon tube. 3. Add 10 ml of 1x PBS and mix gently by inverting 5 times. 4. Add 12 ml of Ficoll to the bottom of a fresh 50 ml Falcon tube. 5. Gently overlay the blood (Step 3) onto the Ficoll (Step 4), without breaking Ficoll’s surface. 6. Centrifuge for 20 minutes at 760 x g, at room temperature. The deceleration must be set to zero. Note: It is important that the deceleration is set to zero to prevent mixing of the plasma and interface layers. 7. Remove as much of the plasma, i.e. the top layer, as soon as possible to reveal the interface containing the white cells. Collect the white cells by pipette aspiration. 8. Transfer the white cells to a fresh 15 ml Falcon tube. 9. Top up to 15 ml with (1x) PBS. 10. Centrifuge for 8 minutes at 350 x g, at room temperature. Note: It is no longer important that the deceleration is set to zero. 11. Carefully remove and discard the supernatant. Retain the pellet. 12. Repeat steps 9-11 two more times. 13. At this point, the protocol may be paused, and the cell pellet may be frozen at -80°C. 14. With wide-bore tips, pipette and transfer the pellet to a 2 ml Eppendorf tube and add 40 µl of proteinase K OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Buffy coat DNA PAGE 3 15. Add 8 µl of RNase A and 300 µl of buffer AL to the pellet solution, and vortex to homogenise. 16. Incubate the homogenised pellet at room temperature for 30 minutes. 17. Add 30 µl of MagAttract Suspension G and 360 µl of Buffer MB to the lysate. 18. Place the tube in the ThermoMixer for shaking and incubation at room temperature for 3 minutes at 1400 rpm. 19. Follow Steps 8a to 15 according to the QIAGEN MagAttract HMW DNA kit manual (pages 16-17). We recommend that the elution is performed with 200 µl of TE. a. Place the tube holder of the MagAttract Magnetic Rack on its magnetic base, wait until bead separation has been completed (~1 minute), and remove the supernatant. b. Add 700 µl Buffer MW1 to the sample and place the tube holder of the MagAttract Magnetic Rack onto the mixer. Incubate at room temperature for 1 minute at 1400 RPM. To increase the efficiency of the wash step, remove the tube holder from magnetic base before adding the wash buffer. Add the wash buffer directly onto the magnetic bead pellet. c. Place the tube holder of the MagAttract Magnetic Rack on its magnetic base, wait until bead separation has been completed (~1 minute), and remove the supernatant. d. Repeat steps b. and c. e. Add 700 µl Buffer PE to the sample and place the tube holder of the MagAttract Magnetic Rack onto the mixer. Incubate at room temperature for 1 minute at 1400 RPM. To increase the efficiency of the wash step, remove the tube holder from the magnetic base before adding the wash buffer. Add the wash buffer directly onto the magnetic bead pellet. f. Place the tube holder of the MagAttract Magnetic Rack on its magnetic base, wait until bead separation has been completed (~1 minute), and remove the supernatant. g. Repeat steps e. and f. h. Rinse the particles with 700 µl distilled water while the tube holder is on the magnetic base and the beads are fixed to the wall of the sample tube. Incubate for 1 minute at room temperature, and remove the supernatant completely. Do not pipette water directly onto the bead pellet. Pipette it into the sample tube against the side facing away from the bead pellet. All pipetting steps must be performed carefully to avoid disturbing the fixed bead pellet. i. Repeat step h. j. Remove the tube holder of the MagAttract Magnetic Rack from its magnetic base and add 200 µl of TE buffer. Place the tube holder onto the mixer and incubate at room temperature for 3 minutes at 1400 RPM. 21. Take 3 µg of eluate and perform a SPRI size selection. Results Yield: 4-6 μg OD 260/280: 1.97 OD 260/230: 2.41 OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Buffy coat DNA PAGE 4 Sequencing performance Libraries were prepared from unsheared and sheared DNA using the Ligation Sequencing Kit. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Buffy coat DNA PAGE 5 Change log Version Change v1, 26th June 2019 Initial protocol publication v2, 14th August 2023 Updated recommended steps to follow in the QIAGEN handbook and wrote out the steps in full. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Buffy coat DNA PAGE 6

docs.title: C elegans dna extraction method
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Worm (Caenorhabditis elegans) DNA FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Worm (Caenorhabditis elegans) DNA PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Methods Results Sequencing performance Change log OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Worm (Caenorhabditis elegans) DNA PAGE 2 Introduction This protocol describes a method to extract high molecular weight genomic DNA from C. elegans, strain SX3254 (descendent from N2 wild type). The worms were grown in NGM (nematode growth media) with E. coli as a food source, and were harvested with M9 buffer (0.85 M Na2HPO4, 0.4 M KH2PO4, 0.3 M NaCl, 1 mM MgSO4) as described this protocol (steps 1 and 2). The extraction was carried out using the QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit and part of the genomic DNA was size-selected with the semi-selective DNA precipitation method. Sequencing performance was assessed using the MinION. Materials Pellet from one plate of C. elegans worms, harvested as described here (steps 1 and 2) and stored at –20°C or –80°C QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit QIAGEN RNase A Proteinase K Isopropanol 70% ethanol in nuclease-free water TE buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) Vortex mixer 15 ml Falcon tubes 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tubes Ice bucket with ice Refrigerated centrifuge and rotor for 15 ml tubes Incubator or water bath with capacity for 37°C and 50°C Sterile paper wipes Qubit dsDNA BR Assay Kit (ThermoFisher Scientific) 70% ethanol in nuclease-free water 2X “size selection buffer” (2.5% w/v PVP 360000 1.2 M NaCl, 20 mM Tris.HCl pH 8) Methods 1. Add 3 ml of Cell Lysis Solution to the 15 ml Falcon tube containing the frozen worm pellet, and allow the pellet to thaw in the lysis buffer. 2. Add 15 µl of Proteinase K and resuspend the pellet by pipetting with a 1 ml wide-bore tip. 3. Incubate the resuspended worms at 50°C for 1 hour; if after 1 hour the lysate is not homogeneous (i.e. if whole worms or pieces of worms are still visible), extend the incubation to 2 hours. During this incubation, gently invert the tube 3 times every 30 minutes. 4. Add 15 µl of RNase A and mix by inverting the tube. 5. Incubate the tube at 37°C for 30 minutes. 6. Place the tube on ice for 2 minutes. 7. Add 1 ml of Protein Precipitation Solution and pulse-vortex three times for 5 seconds. 8. Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 10 minutes. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Worm (Caenorhabditis elegans) DNA PAGE 3 9. Add 3 ml of isopropanol to a fresh 15 ml Falcon tube. 10. Pour the supernatant from step 8 into the Falcon tube with isopropanol. Discard the pellet. 11. Gently invert the tube 50 times. 12. Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 5 minutes. 13. Discard the supernatant and add 3 ml of 70% ice-cold ethanol to the pellet. Gently invert the tube several times to mix. 14. Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 2 minutes. 15. Discard the supernatant and remove as much ethanol as possible using sterile paper wipes. 16. To maximize DNA yield, we recommend that the elution is performed overnight at room temperature in 600 µl TE buffer. 17. Take 3 µg of extracted DNA and perform a size selection using the Size selection of HMW DNA by semi-selective DNA precipitation protocol. The expected DNA recovery after size selection is ~40–50%. Results Yield: 80–90 µg OD260/280: 2.15 OD 260/230: 2.23 OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Worm (Caenorhabditis elegans) DNA PAGE 4 Sequencing performance Libraries were prepared using the Ligation Sequencing Kit. Read length profile: Change log Version Change v3, December 2022 Updated Puregene name and link v2, September 2021 Updated protocol to size select DNA using the size selection of HMW DNA by semi-selective DNA precipitation protocol v1, 19th August 2019 Initial protocol publication OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Worm (Caenorhabditis elegans) DNA PAGE 5

docs.title: Cell free dna cfdna info sheet
docs.text: INFOSHEET Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository V CFD_S1020_v1_revB_10Mar2025 Analysis of cell-free (cf)DNA can be used for a range of uses, including cancer detection and tissue-of-origin analysis. This document is a repository for all of our internally developed methods, know- how documents and information. This will ensure our users can easily access all the required information and our recommended best practices using nanopore sequencing technology for cfDNA. FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 1 Contents Resources 1. Resources OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 2 1. Resources End-to-end protocols Our end-to-end protocols provide you with a step-by-step guide to carry out the preparation and sequencing of a human cfDNA sample from fresh blood or plasma. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 3 Ligation sequencing V14 — Human cfDNA singleplex (SQK-LSK114) Our singleplex method allows you to process a single human cell-free DNA (cfDNA) sample with our Ligation Sequencing Kit V14 (SQK-LSK114). Typically, we obtain ~50 Gb of aligned data (15x coverage) for human cfDNA samples processed with this protocol. Sequencing and primary aligned output for 4x replicates of ~15 ng inputs, with each library run on a single PromethION flow cell. Where a) sequencing output averages 70 Gb, b) primary aligned data averages 52 Gb and c) read count averages 227 M. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 4 Ligation sequencing V14 — Human cfDNA singleplex (SQK-LSK114) Read length profiles (Q-score ≥10) of total data output for a) Sequencing data, b) Aligned data. The marginal reduction in length of aligned data is due to the informatic removal of sequencing adapter. These profiles are equivalent for each of the 3x replicates. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 5 Ligation sequencing V14 — Human cfDNA multiplex (SQK-NBD114.24) Our multiplex method describes how to carry out preparation and sequencing of 12 human cell-free DNA (cfDNA) samples using the Native Barcoding Kit 24 V14 (SQK-NBD114.24). Typically, we obtain ~3 Gb of aligned data (1x coverage) for each of the 12 human cfDNA samples processed with this protocol. Per barcode sequence data output from single PromethION flow cells, using 12x barcoded libraries (72 hour runs) (Q-score ≥10) . a) Raw sequencing data (Gb). b) Primary aligned data (Gb). 6 ng of cfDNA was required to generate >3Gb of aligned data which equates to approximately 1X human genome coverage. c) Read count (million reads). OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 6 Ligation sequencing V14 — Human cfDNA multiplex (SQK-NBD114.24) Read length profiles (Q-score ≥10) of total data output (multiplexed) for a) Sequencing data, b) Aligned data. The reduction in length of aligned data is due to the removal of native barcode and adapter sequence. These profiles are equivalent for each of the 12x demultiplexed samples. Sample extraction methods Our extraction methods have been developed by our internal teams to generate the best input available for your sequencing experiment. These methods use the QIAGEN QIAamp MinElute ccfDNA Midi Kit. Please note, these sample extraction methods have also been fully integrated into our end-to-end protocols. Human blood cell-free DNA (cfDNA) extraction for singleplex sequencing OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 7 Human blood cell-free DNA (cfDNA) extraction for multiplex sequencing Example of fragment length profile of extracted cfDNA using QIAGEN QIAamp MinElute ccfDNA Midi Kit, run on a Femto Pulse (Agilent). This example shows the characteristic nucleosome peaks with minimal gDNA contamination. Know-how and information documents Our know-how and information documents present you with additional background information on the methods we have developed and/or provide additional practices and data to optimise your sequencing experiment. Updated method for cell-free DNA (cfDNA) methylation profiling: Document providing additional information and background on the cfDNA sequencing methods for retaining methylation in cfDNA reads, providing bioinformatic options to omit methylation information at specific read positions, and characterising the loss of methylation for the three most common cfDNA nucleosome lengths. Optimisation of library preparation for longer cell-free DNA (cfDNA) libraries: Study showing that the characteristic length profile of cfDNA can be manipulated by performing size selection during library preparation to enrich for longer fragments. We also show that the fragment length distribution of the sample is a reasonable predictor of both the raw read length and aligned read length profiles observed from sequencing. Assessment of cfDNA extraction kits for nanopore sequencing: Study assessing the performance of commonly used cfDNA extraction kits with regards to cfDNA yield and sequencing performance. Comparative analysis of cfDNA collection tubes for nanopore sequencing: Study evaluating the performance of commonly used blood collection tubes and demonstrating how the choice of collection tube and sample storage time can impact sequencing performance. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Cell free DNA (cfDNA) information repository PAGE 8

docs.title: Cell line dna pure extraction method en 13
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit PAGE 1 Contents Materials Method Results Sequencing performance OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit PAGE 2 Materials 5 x 10⁶ cells QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit 70% ethanol in nuclease-free water TE buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) 1x phosphate buffered saline (PBS) Isopropanol 15 ml Falcon tubes 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tubes Wide-bore pipette tips Centrifuge and rotor for 15 ml Falcon tubes Incubator or water bath set at 37°C and 50°C Vortex mixer Inoculation loop or disposable tweezers for spooling DNA Method 1. Harvest and pellet 5 x 10⁶ cells by centrifugation at 300 x g for 3 minutes. If any liquid remains associated with the pellet, spin down the cells again and aspirate the remaining supernatant. 2. Add 200 µl of 1x PBS to the pelleted cells and centrifuge at 300 x g for 3 minutes. Aspirate and discard the supernatant. 3. Add 2 ml of Cell Lysis Solution to the washed cell pellet. Using a wide-bore pipette tip, resuspend the cells and transfer them to a 15 ml Falcon tube. If clumps of cells remain, gently invert the tube. 4. Incubate the sample at 37°C for 30 minutes. 5. Add 700 µl of the Protein Precipitation Solution to the lysed cells and mix by vortexing for three pulses of 5 seconds. 6. Centrifuge the sample at 2000 x g for 5 minutes. 7. Transfer the supernatant to a new tube and add 2.5 ml of room temperature isopropanol. Discard the pellet. 8. Mix by gently inverting the tube 50 times. 9. Spool the DNA using an inoculation loop or disposable tweezers. 10. Dip the spooled DNA in an Eppendorf tube containing 70% cold ethanol. 11. Remove the inoculation loop or tweezers with the spooled DNA from the ethanol tube, and allow it to air-dry for a few seconds. 12. Dip the DNA in a 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tube containing 250 µl TE (1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) and allow the DNA to gently dislodge from the loop/tweezers. 13. Incubate the DNA pellet for 2 hours at 50°C, occasionally mixing the tube contents by gentle inversion. Note: The pellet may take some time to dissolve, so ensure the solution is homogenous before quantifying. 14. Take 3 µg of eluate and perform a SPRI size selection. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit PAGE 3 Results Yield: 20-30 µg OD 260/280: 1.99 OD 260/230: 2.43 Sequencing performance Libraries were prepared using the Ligation Sequencing Kit: OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit PAGE 4 Version Changelog v3, December 2022 Updated step 1 to include centrifuge recommendations V2, November 2022 Removed Gentra from QIAGEN Puregene kit name and link updated V1 Initial publication OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Puregene Cell Kit PAGE 5

docs.title: Cell line dna qgn extraction method en 13
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Genomic-tip FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Genomic-tip PAGE 1 Contents Materials Method Results Sequencing performance OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Genomic-tip PAGE 2 Materials Cell culture Qiagen Blood and Cell Culture DNA Maxi Kit 70% ethanol in nuclease-free water TE buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) 50 ml Falcon tubes Shaker for Eppendorf tubes Microfuge Method 1. Harvest 1 x 10^8 cells and lyse them according to the QIAGEN Genomic DNA Handbook from the Preparation of cell culture section on page 25. 2. Follow the QIAGEN Genomic-tip handbook, starting on the Isolation of genomic DNA from blood, cultured cells, tissue, yeast, or bacteria using genomic-tips secton on page 49. 3. Optional Step: At the elution stage, use buffer QF warmed up to 50°C, and spin down the precipitated DNA at 4300 g for 15 minutes at 4°C. Wash the pellet with cold 70% ethanol, and spin down at 4400 g for 10 minutes at 4°C. 4. Resuspend the pellet in 1 ml of sterile TE (10 mM Tris-HCl 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) on a platform shaker overnight at room temperature. Results Yield: 400-450 ng/µl OD 260/280: 1.9 OD 260/230: 2.4 OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Genomic-tip PAGE 3 Fragment size (FEMTO pulse): OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Human cell line DNA – QIAGEN Genomic-tip PAGE 4 Sequencing performance Libraries for Nanopore sequencing were prepared using the Ligation Sequencing Kit. docs.text: Chemistry Technical Document Version: CHTD_500_v1_revAU_26May2026 Last update: 5/26/2026 Kit batch number Flow cell number DNA Samples OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Chemistry Technical Document | Oxford Nanopore Technologies PAGE 1

docs.title: Colony pcr dna extraction method
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Whole genome colony PCR FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Whole genome colony PCR PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Methods Sequencing performance Change log OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Whole genome colony PCR PAGE 2 Introduction This protocol describes a method to extract and prepare genomic and plasmid DNA from a bacterial colony. This has been verified using gram-negative E. coli. A colony was picked from a plate of cultured bacteria and then treated with Proteinase K before preparing libraries for sequencing using the Rapid PCR Barcoding Kit. The sequencing performance of the prepared libraries was assessed using GridION. Materials Plated bacterial colonies Rapid PCR Barcoding Kit SFB Expansion (EXP-SFB001) Flow Cell Priming Kit 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tubes 0.2 ml thin-walled PCR tubes Nuclease-free water (e.g. ThermoFisher, cat # AM9937) Agencourt AMPure XP beads LongAmp Taq 2X Master Mix (e.g. NEB M0287) 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0, 50mM NaCl (or EB from Sequencing Auxillary Vials (EXP-AUX001 or equivalent) (Optional) Thermolabile Proteinase K (e.g. NEB P8111S) Microfuge Thermal cycler Pipettes and tips for P1000, P200, P100, P20, P10, and P2 Timer Ice bucket with ice Magnetic separator, suitable for either 1.5 ml Eppendorf tubes or 0.2 ml PCR tubes Hula mixer (or similar gentle rotator mixer) DNA QC equipment, e.g. Agilent Bioanalyzer, Qubit fluorometer Inoculating loop/needle or sterile toothpick Methods 1. Using either a pipette tip, sterile toothpick, inoculating loop or needle, pick one colony and dip into 50 μl of 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0. Swirl for 10 seconds until the solution becomes turbid. 2. Enriching for plasmid DNA only: If you are interested in the genomic DNA, proceed straight to step 4. If you are interested in the plasmid DNA, transfer the 50 μl cell suspension to a 0.2 ml thin-walled PCR tube and incubate at 95°C for 5 minutes. Note: It is observed that pre-treating the colony by heating leads to an enrichment in the observation of plasmid reads in the downstream sequencing, compared with non-heat treated libraries. 3. Enriching for plasmid DNA only: Add 1 μl of Thermolabile Proteinase K and incubate at 37°C for 15 minutes, then 55°C for 10 minutes. 4. Libraries can be prepared for sequencing using the Rapid PCR Barcoding Kit, using 3 μl of the treated cell suspension as the template. If using cells without the heat treatment in step two, it OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Whole genome colony PCR PAGE 3 is recommended to PCR for 25 cycles, and if using heat-treated cells, it is recommended to PCR for 30 cycles. Sequencing performance Libraries were prepared using the Rapid PCR Barcoding Kit: Post PCR yield: 30-60 ng/μl Output from the flow cell may be increased by performing a flow cell wash step at the point where the rate of data acquisition begins to deteriorate due to the accumulation of pores in the “unavailable” or “recovering” state, and then adding a new library. Read length profile: Change log Version Change v3, 02nd April 2024 Removed reference to Exonuclease I in Equipment and consumables as no longer needed with the V14 Rapid PCR Barcoding Kit v2, 21st April 2021 Title changed from ‘PCR amplification of gram-negative bacterial DNA direct from a colony’ to ‘Whole genome colony PCR’. Updated step 3 sub-title from ‘(plasmid DNA only)’ to ‘(enriching for plasmid DNA only)’. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Whole genome colony PCR PAGE 4 OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Whole genome colony PCR PAGE 5

docs.title: Covaris g tube dna extraction method
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Shearing genomic DNA using the Covaris g-TUBE™ FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Shearing genomic DNA using the Covaris g-TUBE™ PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Method Sequencing performance Change log OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Shearing genomic DNA using the Covaris g-TUBE™ PAGE 2 Introduction By default, Ligation Sequencing Kit protocols contain no designated fragmentation step. However fragmentation is optional for all users, and is recommended when working with lower amounts of input gDNA (100 ng–500 ng), where fragmentation will increase the number of DNA molecules, and therefore increase throughput. The standard Covaris g-TUBE protocol refers to using >4 µg genomic DNA. However, development work at Oxford Nanopore has shown that 100–1000 ng genomic DNA in 49 µl fragments in the same way. Oxford Nanopore routinely uses an Eppendorf 5424 microfuge at 6000 rpm to generate average Lambda DNA fragments of 8 kb. When using different genomic DNA samples, wanting different fragment sizes and using different centrifuges, optimisation of spin speeds may be required. Please refer to the g-TUBE™ literature for more details. Materials gDNA in 49 µl nuclease-free water Covaris g-TUBE™ Microfuge 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tubes DNA QC equipment, e.g. Qubit fluorometer, NanoDrop spectrophotometer, Agilent Bioanalyzer or Tapestation, Agilent FEMTO Pulse Method 1. Transfer 100 ng–1000 ng genomic DNA into a 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tube, and adjust the volume to 49 μl with nuclease-free water. Mix the DNA thoroughly by flicking the tube. Spin down briefly in a microfuge. 2. Transfer the genomic DNA sample in 49 μl to a Covaris g-TUBE™. 3. Centrifuge the g-TUBE™ for one minute at room temperature at the speed for the fragment size required. Remove and check that all the DNA has passed through the tube. 4. Optional step: If DNA remains in the upper chamber, spin again for one minute at the same speed. 5. Invert the g-TUBE™ and centrifuge again for one minute to collect the fragmented DNA. Remove and check that all the DNA has passed through the tube. 6. Optional step: If DNA remains in the upper chamber, spin again for one minute at the same speed. 7. Transfer the 49 μl fragmented DNA into a clean 1.5 ml Eppendorf DNA LoBind tube. 8. Analyse 1 μl of the fragmented DNA for fragment size, quality and quantity. Sequencing performance Libraries were prepared using the Ligation Sequencing Kit (SQK-LSK109). Read length profile: OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Shearing genomic DNA using the Covaris g-TUBE™ PAGE 3 Change log Version Change v1, September 2019 Initial protocol publication OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Shearing genomic DNA using the Covaris g-TUBE™ PAGE 4

docs.title: Dry blood spots dna extraction method
docs.text: OXFORD NANOPORE Automated gDNA extraction from dry blood spots (FTA cards) FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Automated gDNA extraction from dry blood spots (FTA cards) PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Method Results OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Automated gDNA extraction from dry blood spots (FTA cards) PAGE 2 Introduction This protocol describes a method to extract genomic DNA (gDNA) from human blood collected on Whatman’s Flinders Technology Associates (FTA) cards. The blood was collected in EDTA K2 solution and applied to the FTA cards upon arrival in the laboratory. The blood cells were counted before application to the FTA cards to assess the efficiency of this method. Optimal results were obtained using 10 discs of 3 mm diameter each, pre-lysed for one hour. The discs were loaded into the QIAGEN EZ2 Connect automation device using the tissue protocol for 15 minutes to complete purification. Different numbers of discs were tested and results showed 10 discs was the maximum which can be used whilst still obtaining consistent DNA recovery. The Ligation Sequencing Kit was used to generate sequencing libraries from the extracted DNA, and the libraries were sequenced on the PromethION device. The QIAGEN MagAttract kit may also be a suitable alternative to extract the gDNA as it is based on the same principle of magnetic beads, but this method has not been tested. Materials Blood collected on FTA cards 3 mm puncher Cutting mat Tweezer Thermomixer, or incubator with heating capacity of 56°C and agitation capacity of 900 rpm 2 ml DNA LoBind Eppendorf tubes QIAGEN EZ2 Connect automation device EZ 1 & 2 DNA Tissue Kit Method 1. Apply the blood to the FTA card and allow the sample to completely dry. 2. Place the FTA card on the cutting mat and cut out 10 discs using a 3 mm puncher. 3. Using tweezers, transfer the 10 discs to a 2 ml DNA LoBind Eppendorf tube. 4. Add 290 µl of G2 buffer and 10 µl of proteinase K. 5. Incubate the sample on a thermomixer, or equivalent, for 1 hour at 56°C and 900 rpm. 6. Remove the tube from the thermomixer and transfer the lysate to the sample tube provided in the kit by pipetting. Transfer as much lysate as possible, being careful not to transfer any FTA discs as these can block the instrument's tips. 7. Set up the automation device according to the manufacturer’s instructions, select the tissues protocol and set the elution volume to 50 µl. Results Typical protocol yield: 200-450 ng The DNA library for sequencing was prepared using the Ligation Sequencing Kit. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Automated gDNA extraction from dry blood spots (FTA cards) PAGE 3 Bases sequenced (Gb ) Read length (kb) 0 10 30 50 70 0.0 0.4 0.6 0.2 1.0 0.8 20 40 60 Figure 1. Read length analysis of the extracted gDNA from human blood samples. Output (Gb ) Run time (h) 0 30 40 10 70 50 20 0 60 80 40 20 120 100 60 Figure 2. Output analysis of the extracted gDNA from human blood samples. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Automated gDNA extraction from dry blood spots (FTA cards) PAGE 4

docs.title: Environmental water edna extraction method
docs.text:
OXFORD NANOPORE Environmental water (eDNA) FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Environmental water (eDNA) PAGE 1 Contents Introduction Materials Method Results Sequencing performance Species identification OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Environmental water (eDNA) PAGE 2 Introduction This protocol describes a method to extract genomic DNA (gDNA) from pond water as an example of an environmental water sample. We tested multiple extraction kits and compared performance using ZymoBIOMICS Microbial Community Standard, a commercial sample of known composition. Our initial experiments were carried out using ZymoBIOMICS Microbial Community Standard diluted in sterile water and filtered through a vacuum before the gDNA was extracted directly from the filter. We tested the extraction kits: ZymoBIOMICS DNA Miniprep Kit Zymo Quick-DNA HMW MagBead Kit QIAGEN MagAttract QIAGEN Puregene QIAGEN PowerWater The extraction kit that yielded the community representation closest to the expected control was ZymoBIOMICS DNA Miniprep Kit. All the other methods showed a deviation from the expected species distribution. To validate the method further, we used a sample of water from a residential garden pond (containing a known fish species). The sample was extracted and prepared for sequencing with and without PCR amplification using the Rapid PCR Barcoding Kit and the Ligation Sequencing Kit, respectively. DNA was also amplified using QIAGEN REPLI-G Midi kit for whole genome amplification following our recommended protocol; Ligation sequencing gDNA - whole genome amplification. All samples were sequenced on MinION and the reads generated were analysed using Centrifuge run on raw reads. Results showed that the expected fish species were detected in all samples. In addition, it was also possible to detect other eukaryotic species that are present in the garden such as rodents, birds, and plants, alongside diverse species belonging to the rich microbial community that can be found in an environmental sample. Materials ZymoBIOMICS Microbial Community Standard ZymoBIOMICS DNA Miniprep Kit Vacuum filter unit (e.g. Thermo Scientific™ Nalgene™ Reusable Filter Units) Vacuum pump 0.2 µm cellulose nitrate filter (e.g. Cytiva Whatman™ Cellulose Nitrate Membrane Filters) ZR BashingBead Lysis Tubes Tweezers 1.5 ml Eppendorf tubes Vortex mixer Microcentrifuge Method 1. Prepare the vacuum filter unit with a 0.2 µm cellulose nitrate filter. 2. Pour 500 ml of pond water through a vacuum filter unit. OXFORD NANOPORE TECHNOLOGIES | Environmental water (eDNA) PAGE 3 3. Once all the solution has passed through vacuum, disassemble the vacuum filter unit, and use tweezers to remove the filter. 4. Carefully roll the filter with tweezers and transfer it directly to a ZR BashingBead™ Lysis Tube. 5. Add 750 µl of ZymoBIOMICS™ Lysis solution, and ensure the tube is properly closed. 6. Place the tube in a tube holder, vortex adapter or simply tape the tubes to the vortex. Agitate at maximum speed for 40 minutes. 7. Follow the recommended protocol from steps 4-13 (pages 6-7 from the ZymoBIOMICS DNA Miniprep kit handbook.
